beginner.title: Who Are You?
beginner.target_grammar: ser vs estar introduction — ser for identity
beginner.topic: Introducing yourself — name, origin, basic identity
beginner.why: Survival skill #1. Establishes the ser/estar distinction from day one so it becomes instinct, not a rule to memorize.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor introduces self in Spanish, asks learner to repeat pattern
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Model 'Me llamo ___, soy de ___' and have learner fill in their own info. Keep ser for identity only — do not introduce estar yet. Praise any attempt at Spanish. Correct pronunciation gently by repeating the correct form naturally.
assessment_targets: Can state name using me llamo
Can state origin using soy de
Responds to mucho gusto appropriately

beginner.title: At the Coffee Shop
beginner.target_grammar: quiero + noun, por favor / gracias
beginner.topic: Ordering food and drinks — basic transactional Spanish
beginner.why: Immediate practical payoff. Quiero + noun is the simplest sentence pattern and builds confidence for real-world use.
beginner.warm_up: Roleplay: tutor is the waiter, greets with Buenas tardes, que desea?
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Run a cafe roleplay. Accept 'quiero + noun' as the primary pattern. Introduce menu items naturally. If learner uses English for a food word, provide the Spanish and have them retry. End with paying — la cuenta, por favor.
assessment_targets: Can order using quiero + noun
Uses por favor and gracias
Can request the check

beginner.title: Numbers, Prices, and Haggling
beginner.target_grammar: cuanto cuesta + noun, numbers 1-100
beginner.topic: Numbers in context — prices, ages, phone numbers
beginner.why: Numbers are boring in isolation. Anchoring them to prices and ages gives them meaning. Cuanto cuesta is immediately useful.
beginner.warm_up: Quick-fire number recognition: tutor says numbers, learner repeats
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Drill numbers through a market roleplay. Quote prices, ask learner to repeat. Introduce cuanto cuesta for asking prices. Practice age — cuantos anos tienes? Keep energy high, this is a drill-heavy lesson. Use chunking: 1-10, then 11-20, then tens.
assessment_targets: Can count 1-20 fluently
Can ask cuanto cuesta
Can state their age

beginner.title: My Family, My People
beginner.target_grammar: tener for possession and age, possessive adjectives mi/tu/su
beginner.topic: Describing your family — who they are, how old, what they are like
beginner.why: Family vocabulary is high-frequency and emotionally resonant. Tener is the second most important verb after ser/estar.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor describes their own (fictional) family, asks learner questions
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Have learner describe their family using tengo + number + hermanos. Introduce possessive adjectives naturally (mi mama, tu papa). If learner is heritage, they may already know these words — push for full sentences and correct grammar. This is a good diagnostic lesson.
assessment_targets: Can name family members
Uses tener for age correctly
Can form possessive phrases with mi/tu

beginner.title: Where Is It? Navigating Space
beginner.target_grammar: estar for location, basic prepositions
beginner.topic: Asking for and giving directions, describing locations
beginner.why: Completes the ser/estar split — now estar has a clear function (location). Prepositions unlock spatial language.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor hides an object in a described room, learner asks donde esta
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Contrast with ser from lesson 1: soy de Mexico (origin/identity) vs estoy en la biblioteca (current location). Use a map or room description. Practice donde esta + noun. Introduce prepositions through a treasure hunt game. Correct ser/estar confusion by restating with emphasis.
assessment_targets: Uses estar for location
Can ask donde esta
Knows at least 3 prepositions of place

beginner.title: What Do You Do Every Day?
beginner.target_grammar: present tense regular -ar verbs, daily routine vocabulary
beginner.topic: Describing daily routines and habits
beginner.why: First systematic verb conjugation. -ar verbs are the largest class and the most regular. Daily routines provide natural repetition context.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor describes their morning routine, asks learner que haces por la manana?
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Focus on yo and tu forms of -ar verbs. Build sentences around learner's actual routine. Introduce time expressions (por la manana, por la noche, todos los dias). Do not overwhelm with full conjugation tables — keep it to yo/tu/el-ella for now. Correct endings gently.
assessment_targets: Can conjugate 3+ -ar verbs in yo form
Can describe morning routine in 3+ sentences
Uses at least one time expression

beginner.title: I Like, I Don't Like
beginner.target_grammar: gustar construction (me gusta / no me gusta), indirect object pronouns intro
beginner.topic: Expressing preferences — food, music, activities, hobbies
beginner.why: Gustar is deceptively tricky (backward construction). Early exposure prevents fossilized errors. Also unlocks opinion-giving, which makes conversation real.
beginner.warm_up: Rapid preference game: tutor names items, learner says me gusta or no me gusta
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach gustar as a fixed chunk first (me gusta + noun). Do NOT explain indirect objects grammatically — let the pattern sink in through repetition. Contrast me gusta (singular thing) vs me gustan (plural things) once learner is comfortable. Use learner's real interests to keep it engaging.
assessment_targets: Can express likes with me gusta + noun
Can express dislikes with no me gusta
Attempts me gustan for plurals

beginner.title: What Did You Do Yesterday?
beginner.target_grammar: preterite tense — regular -ar verbs, high-frequency irregulars (fui, comi, hice)
beginner.topic: Talking about completed past actions — yesterday's events
beginner.why: Past tense is the gateway to storytelling. Starting with high-frequency irregulars (fui, hice) rather than regular conjugation tables mirrors natural acquisition.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor asks que hiciste ayer? and accepts any attempt
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach the most common irregular preterites as vocabulary chunks (fui = I went, comi = I ate, hice = I did). Do not teach regular preterite conjugation tables yet — focus on the 5 most useful irregular forms. Have learner narrate yesterday using these chunks. Gently recast present tense attempts into preterite.
assessment_targets: Can use fui, comi, or hice in context
Can narrate 3+ past events
Recognizes ayer/anoche as past time markers

beginner.title: Making Plans
beginner.target_grammar: ir + a + infinitive for future, querer + infinitive
beginner.topic: Talking about future plans and intentions
beginner.why: Ir + a + infinitive is how most native speakers express future in conversation. It is more useful than the synthetic future tense at this level.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor shares weekend plans, asks y tu, que vas a hacer?
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach voy a + infinitive as the primary future construction. Combine with quiero + infinitive for wants. Practice by planning a fictional trip or weekend together. Introduce vamos a for 'let's' / 'we're going to'. This lesson should feel like a conversation, not a drill.
assessment_targets: Can form voy a + infinitive sentences
Can state wants with quiero + infinitive
Can discuss weekend plans in 4+ sentences

beginner.title: Tell Me a Story
beginner.target_grammar: combining present, past, and future; connectors (pero, porque, entonces, despues)
beginner.topic: Narrating a sequence of events — combining all tenses learned
beginner.why: Capstone lesson. Forces integration of everything learned. Connectors transform choppy sentences into coherent narrative, which is the real A2 milestone.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor tells a short anecdote using connectors, asks learner to retell it
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Have learner tell a story — what they did last week, a funny memory, or a fictional tale. Push for connectors (porque, pero, entonces) to link ideas. Gently correct tense mixing only when it causes confusion. Celebrate fluency over accuracy in this lesson. This is a progress check — note which areas need review.
assessment_targets: Uses at least 2 connectors in narration
Combines past and present/future in story
Can sustain a 6+ sentence narrative

heritage.title: Talking About Your Day
heritage.target_grammar: preterite vs imperfect basics
heritage.topic: What you did today — food, activities, feelings
heritage.why: Forces past tense usage naturally through personal storytelling. Heritage speakers often mix tenses — this surfaces the pattern for cleanup.
heritage.warm_up: Ask about today's meals and activities in full Spanish
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Guide conversation toward describing the day. Use preterite for completed actions, introduce imperfect for background descriptions. Listen for tense-mixing patterns common in heritage speakers (using present for past, or avoiding imperfect entirely). Correct verb tense errors gently by recasting. Do not lecture on grammar rules — model correct usage.
assessment_targets: Uses preterite for completed actions
Attempts past tense conjugation
Can name 3+ foods in Spanish

heritage.title: The Doctor's Office
heritage.target_grammar: me duele / tengo dolor de, body parts, formal register
heritage.topic: Describing symptoms, making medical appointments, understanding prescriptions
heritage.why: George is pre-med. Medical Spanish is directly career-relevant. Heritage speakers often lack formal/technical medical vocabulary even if they can chat fluently.
heritage.warm_up: Scenario: you are calling to make a doctor's appointment for your abuela who does not speak English
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Roleplay medical scenarios. Push beyond kitchen Spanish into formal register. Introduce body part vocabulary through symptoms (me duele la cabeza, tengo fiebre). Practice making appointments by phone. Contrast informal (me siento mal) with clinical (presento sintomas de). This is high-stakes vocabulary — treat it seriously.
assessment_targets: Can describe 3+ symptoms using me duele / tengo
Uses formal register for appointment-making
Knows 5+ body parts in Spanish

heritage.title: Texting vs Talking vs Writing
heritage.target_grammar: register awareness — informal, semi-formal, formal
heritage.topic: How Spanish changes by context — texts to friends, emails to professors, speaking to elders
heritage.why: Heritage speakers often have one register (family informal). Unlocking formal register is essential for professional and academic Spanish.
heritage.warm_up: Show same message in 3 registers: text to friend, email to professor, letter to government office
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Present the same content in three registers and have learner identify differences. Practice writing a formal email (Estimado profesor, le escribo para...). Contrast tu vs usted systematically. Heritage speakers often default to tu — practice usted conjugations in realistic scenarios (talking to a doctor, a judge, an elderly stranger). Praise register-switching ability.
assessment_targets: Can identify informal vs formal register
Uses usted correctly in formal context
Can write opening of a formal email

heritage.title: Reading the News in Spanish
heritage.target_grammar: passive voice recognition, journalistic vocabulary
heritage.topic: Reading and discussing news articles — politics, sports, local events
heritage.why: Heritage speakers can often speak but not read fluently. News reading builds literacy, exposes academic vocabulary, and connects to real-world content.
heritage.warm_up: Read a headline together: tutor reads, learner interprets meaning
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Use a real or realistic news snippet. Read it aloud together. Check comprehension — can learner identify who, what, where, when? Introduce journalistic vocabulary (segun fuentes, se reporto que). Discuss the topic in conversational Spanish afterward. This bridges reading comprehension to speaking. Note any words learner can say but not read.
assessment_targets: Can read a news headline aloud with correct pronunciation
Can summarize a short news paragraph
Recognizes 3+ journalistic expressions

heritage.title: Subjunctive Without Fear
heritage.target_grammar: present subjunctive after quiero que, espero que, es importante que
heritage.topic: Expressing wishes, hopes, and recommendations about other people's actions
heritage.why: Heritage speakers often use subjunctive correctly in fixed phrases but cannot produce it intentionally. Making it conscious transforms passive knowledge into active skill.
heritage.warm_up: Tutor says 'Quiero que me digas algo interesante de tu semana' — asks learner to notice the verb form
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Do NOT start with grammar explanation. Start with examples the learner likely already says (espero que estes bien, quiero que vengas). Point out the pattern: after quiero que / espero que, the verb changes. Have learner produce sentences about what they want others to do. If they already use subjunctive naturally, push to new contexts (recommendations, doubt). If they avoid it, build from familiar phrases outward.
assessment_targets: Recognizes subjunctive in familiar phrases
Can produce quiero que + subjunctive
Distinguishes indicative from subjunctive in context

heritage.title: Spelling and Accents Matter
heritage.target_grammar: accent rules, b/v confusion, s/c/z distinction, h rules
heritage.topic: Spanish spelling conventions, common heritage speaker errors, accent marks
heritage.why: Heritage speakers write phonetically (aser instead of hacer, bamos instead of vamos). Spelling cleanup is critical for academic and professional credibility.
heritage.warm_up: Dictation exercise: tutor reads 5 sentences, learner writes them
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Run a dictation and check spelling. Focus on the top heritage-speaker spelling issues: h (hacer/aser), b/v (vamos/bamos), c/s/z (cocina/cosina), accent marks (papa vs papa). Do not shame — these are predictable L1 transfer patterns. Teach rules as shortcuts, not corrections. Practice with words from learner's daily life. Introduce accent rules through minimal pairs (si/si, el/el, papa/papa).
assessment_targets: Spells 5+ commonly confused words correctly
Can place accent marks on 3+ words
Identifies own common spelling errors

heritage.title: Beyond Kitchen Spanish
heritage.target_grammar: academic and professional vocabulary domains
heritage.topic: Science and medical vocabulary — expanding beyond household register
heritage.why: Heritage speakers plateau at household vocabulary. George is pre-med — scientific Spanish opens career doors (medical interpretation, research in Latin America, patient communication).
heritage.warm_up: Describe a biology concept in English, then attempt it in Spanish together
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Take a concept George knows in English from his pre-med studies (cell biology, basic chemistry, anatomy) and build the Spanish vocabulary around it. Many scientific terms are cognates (celula, hipotesis, organismo) — point this out to build confidence. Practice explaining a scientific concept in Spanish as if to a patient or student. This is domain expansion, not grammar work.
assessment_targets: Can name 5+ scientific terms in Spanish
Can explain one science concept in simple Spanish
Recognizes cognate patterns between English and Spanish scientific vocabulary

heritage.title: Debating and Defending Opinions
heritage.target_grammar: opinion structures, conditional for hypotheticals, concession (aunque, sin embargo)
heritage.topic: Arguing a position — agreeing, disagreeing, making concessions
heritage.why: Heritage speakers can chat but often cannot argue formally in Spanish. Debate structures build academic register and critical thinking in the language.
heritage.warm_up: Hot take: tutor makes a controversial but lighthearted claim, learner must disagree in Spanish
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Pick a debatable topic (best food, should college be free, is homework useful). Model opinion structures: creo que, en mi opinion, no estoy de acuerdo porque. Push for concession language (sin embargo, aunque tienes razon). Have learner argue both sides. This lesson is about structured argumentation, not vocabulary. Encourage complex sentences.
assessment_targets: Uses creo que / en mi opinion to state position
Can disagree politely with no estoy de acuerdo
Uses at least one concession marker (aunque, sin embargo)

heritage.title: Literature Tasting
heritage.target_grammar: literary past tenses, narrative voice recognition, figurative language
heritage.topic: Reading short literary passages — Borges, Marquez, Cisneros, Neruda
heritage.why: George likes literature. Reading literary Spanish exposes sophisticated grammar in context and builds cultural connection. Heritage speakers rarely get literature exposure in Spanish.
heritage.warm_up: Read opening paragraph of a short story together, discuss what they notice
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Select a short passage (5-10 sentences) from an accessible Latin American author. Read aloud together. Discuss content first (what happened?), then language (what verb tenses? what imagery?). Introduce literary vocabulary (personaje, trama, metafora). Connect to English literature concepts learner already knows. This should feel like a book club, not a class.
assessment_targets: Can read literary Spanish aloud
Can summarize a short passage in own words
Uses 2+ literary analysis terms

heritage.title: Cultural Code-Switching
heritage.target_grammar: Spanglish analysis, code-switching patterns, dialectal awareness
heritage.topic: Understanding your own linguistic identity — Spanglish, regional dialects, when to switch and when not to
heritage.why: Capstone for heritage track. Validates learner's existing bilingualism while building conscious control over register and dialect switching. Identity-affirming and linguistically sophisticated.
heritage.warm_up: Conversation about when learner uses Spanish vs English vs Spanglish and why
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: This is a reflective and analytical lesson. Discuss code-switching: when is it effective communication vs when does it signal a vocabulary gap? Explore regional differences (Mexican vs Caribbean vs Peninsular Spanish). Validate Spanglish as a legitimate linguistic phenomenon while building awareness of when formal monolingual Spanish is needed. Have learner translate a Spanglish conversation into formal Spanish. End with goal-setting for continued Spanish development.
assessment_targets: Can identify own code-switching patterns
Can rephrase a Spanglish sentence in formal Spanish
Shows awareness of at least 2 regional dialect differences

beginner.title: Marhaba! First Words
beginner.target_grammar: basic greetings, masculine/feminine agreement in greetings
beginner.topic: Greetings, pleasantries, yes/no — surviving the first 30 seconds
beginner.why: Arabic greetings are ritualized and culturally loaded. Getting them right builds respect and confidence immediately. Gender agreement starts here.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor greets with as-salamu alaykum, teaches the response, practices 3 times
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach the greeting-response pairs as chunks. Explain that Arabic greetings often have specific responses (as-salamu alaykum -> wa alaykum as-salam). Practice multiple greetings for different formality levels. Introduce keefak (m) / keefik (f) to surface gender agreement early. Use transliteration freely — no Arabic script pressure in lesson 1.
assessment_targets: Can respond to as-salamu alaykum correctly
Uses shukran and afwan appropriately
Can say keefak/keefik with correct gender

beginner.title: The Arabic Alphabet — Part 1
beginner.target_grammar: letters alif through zay, initial/medial/final forms concept
beginner.topic: First 11 letters of the Arabic alphabet — recognition and basic writing
beginner.why: Script is the biggest barrier. Starting early and going slow prevents overwhelm. Connecting letters to already-known words (baba, jamal) makes it stick.
beginner.warm_up: Show letters with familiar words: ba = baba (dad), jim = jamal (camel)
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach 11 letters with mnemonic associations. Focus on recognition first, production second. Show that letters change form based on position (initial, medial, final) but do not require memorization of all forms yet — just awareness. Practice by identifying letters in simple words. Keep it visual and pattern-based. End with learner writing their name in Arabic letters if possible.
assessment_targets: Can recognize 8+ of the first 11 letters
Understands that letters change form by position
Can sound out a 3-letter word

beginner.title: The Arabic Alphabet — Part 2
beginner.target_grammar: letters sin through ya, vowel marks (fatha, kasra, damma)
beginner.topic: Remaining Arabic letters and introduction to short vowels
beginner.why: Completing the alphabet unlocks reading. Short vowel marks are the key to pronunciation — without them, Arabic words are ambiguous.
beginner.warm_up: Review first 11 letters with flashcard-style drill, then introduce new letters in groups of 3
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Complete the alphabet, grouping similar-looking letters (sin/shin, sad/dad). Introduce the three short vowel marks (fatha = a, kasra = i, damma = u) as pronunciation guides. Practice reading simple voweled words. Emphasize the emphatic letters (sad, dad, ta, dha) and the sounds that do not exist in English (ayn, ghayn, qaf). Use audio modeling heavily.
assessment_targets: Can recognize all 28 letters
Can identify the 3 short vowel marks
Can read a 3-letter voweled word

beginner.title: Ana min... — Where Are You From?
beginner.target_grammar: nominal sentences (no verb 'to be'), ana/anta/anti pronouns, nationalities
beginner.topic: Introducing yourself — name, origin, occupation, basic description
beginner.why: Arabic has no present tense 'to be' — nominal sentences are fundamental and completely different from English/Spanish structure. Early exposure prevents confusion.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor models: ana [name], ana min [country], ana [occupation]. Learner repeats with own info.
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach the nominal sentence pattern: ana + noun/adjective (no 'am' needed). Practice with nationalities and occupations. Introduce gender agreement on adjectives/nationalities (amriki/amrikiyya, talib/taliba). Contrast with English: 'I am a student' = 'ana talib' (literally 'I student'). This is a paradigm shift — make it explicit. Use both transliteration and Arabic script.
assessment_targets: Can introduce self using ana + noun pattern
Applies gender agreement to nationality/occupation
Understands that Arabic has no 'to be' in present

beginner.title: At the Suq — Shopping and Bargaining
beginner.target_grammar: numbers 1-20, bikam (how much), demonstratives hada/hadi
beginner.topic: Shopping at a market — asking prices, pointing at things, basic numbers
beginner.why: Market scenarios are culturally authentic for Arabic and provide natural context for numbers, demonstratives, and transactional language.
beginner.warm_up: Market roleplay: tutor is vendor, displays items with prices, learner browses
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach numbers 1-10 first, then 11-20. Practice through a market roleplay using bikam hada/hadi (how much is this m/f). Introduce ureed (I want) for requesting items. Teach ghali (expensive) and rakhis (cheap) for bargaining. Gender agreement on hada/hadi reinforces the masculine/feminine distinction. Keep it lively and transactional.
assessment_targets: Can count 1-10 in Arabic
Can ask bikam with correct demonstrative
Can use ureed to request an item

beginner.title: My Family Tree
beginner.target_grammar: possessive suffixes (-i, -ak, -ik), family vocabulary, idafa construct intro
beginner.topic: Describing your family — relationships, ages, who lives where
beginner.why: Family is central to Arabic culture and conversation. Possessive suffixes are fundamental Arabic grammar that attach to nouns — radically different from English.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor draws a family tree, labels in Arabic, asks learner to describe their own
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach core family vocabulary first, then add possessive suffixes (abi = my father, akhuk = your brother m, akhuik = your brother f). Build sentences: abi min [place], ummi tabakha (my mom cooks). Introduce the idafa (possessive construct) through family: bayt al-jaddi (grandfather's house). Connect to learner's actual family to make it personal.
assessment_targets: Can name 5+ family members in Arabic
Uses possessive suffix -i (my) correctly on 3+ nouns
Can describe family using 3+ sentences

beginner.title: What Time Is It? Daily Schedule
beginner.target_grammar: telling time, present tense verbs (Group I), daily activities
beginner.topic: Describing daily routine — when you wake, eat, study, sleep
beginner.why: First real verb conjugation lesson. Arabic verbs are built on root patterns — introducing this through daily routine verbs reveals the system naturally.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor asks: mata tastayqidh? (when do you wake up?) — teaches the question and helps answer
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Introduce present tense through daily routine verbs. Show the ana (I) form pattern for Group I verbs: adrus (I study), akul (I eat), anam (I sleep). Teach time-telling with as-sa'a + number. Build a daily schedule: as-sa'a sab'a, astayqidh (at 7, I wake up). Point out the root pattern system (d-r-s = study: adrus, madrasa, mudarris) to build morphological awareness.
assessment_targets: Can tell time for 3+ times of day
Can conjugate 3+ verbs in ana (I) form
Can describe daily schedule in 4+ sentences

beginner.title: Root Magic — How Arabic Words Work
beginner.target_grammar: trilateral root system, pattern recognition across word families
beginner.topic: Understanding the Arabic root system — the skeleton key to vocabulary
beginner.why: The root system is Arabic's superpower. Once learner sees the pattern, every new word connects to a family. This lesson is a force multiplier for all future vocabulary.
beginner.warm_up: Word family detective: given kataba (he wrote), what might kitab and maktaba mean?
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: This is a revelation lesson. Take 3 roots (k-t-b, d-r-s, a-k-l) and show how each generates a family of related words through patterns. Let learner guess meanings based on root recognition. Show the patterns: ma-___-a = place of, mu-___-is = person who does, ___-a = the act of. This should feel like cracking a code. Encourage learner to look for roots in words they already know. High engagement, low pressure.
assessment_targets: Can identify the root in 3+ Arabic words
Can guess meaning of a new word from known root
Understands place (ma-) and agent (mu-) patterns

beginner.title: In the Restaurant
beginner.target_grammar: imperative forms, polite requests with law samaht, food vocabulary
beginner.topic: Ordering food, making polite requests, understanding a menu
beginner.why: Restaurant scenarios combine transactional language, politeness formulas, and cultural food vocabulary. Builds on numbers and shopping skills from earlier.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor presents Arabic restaurant menu, learner identifies any words they recognize
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Build on lesson 5's shopping skills. Teach law samaht (please/excuse me) as the politeness formula. Practice ordering: law samaht, ureed dajaj mashwi ma'a ruz. Introduce the imperative a'tini (give me) for direct requests. Practice the full restaurant script: greeting, ordering, asking for water, requesting the check. Introduce common Arabic dishes and cooking terms.
assessment_targets: Can order a full meal in Arabic
Uses law samaht for polite requests
Knows 5+ food vocabulary items

beginner.title: Tell Me About Your Week
beginner.target_grammar: past tense (Group I), days of the week, connecting ideas with wa/thumma/ba'da
beginner.topic: Narrating past events — what happened this week, combining past tense with sequence markers
beginner.why: Capstone lesson. Past tense in Arabic follows the root system beautifully. Combining past narration with connectors tests all accumulated skills.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor narrates their week in simple Arabic, learner picks out verbs and time expressions
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Introduce past tense for ana (I) form: dharastu (I studied), akaltu (I ate), dhahbtu (I went). Show how past tense builds from the root (d-r-s -> dharastu). Teach days of the week. Have learner narrate their actual week using past tense + days + connectors (wa = and, thumma = then, ba'da dhalika = after that). This is a comprehensive review lesson — note gaps for future work.
assessment_targets: Can conjugate 3+ verbs in past tense ana form
Knows at least 4 days of the week
Can narrate a sequence of 4+ past events with connectors

heritage.title: Remembering the Sounds
heritage.target_grammar: pharyngeal and emphatic sounds review, listening discrimination
heritage.topic: Reconnecting with Arabic sounds that faded — the hard consonants
heritage.why: Heritage refreshers often remember vocabulary but lose the difficult sounds (ayn, ha, emphatics). Sound discrimination is the foundation everything else builds on.
heritage.warm_up: Minimal pair listening: tutor says pairs like hasab/Hasab, salam/Salam — can learner hear the difference?
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: This is an ear-training lesson. Play minimal pairs that differ only in emphatic vs plain consonants or pharyngeal vs non-pharyngeal. Start with recognition (can you hear the difference?), then production (try saying both). Be patient — these sounds require reactivation for heritage speakers who grew up hearing but not producing them. Use familiar words that contain these sounds. Celebrate small improvements.
assessment_targets: Can hear difference between plain and emphatic consonants in 3+ pairs
Can produce ayn sound
Can distinguish ha from kha in context

heritage.title: I Can Talk but I Can't Read
heritage.target_grammar: Arabic script recognition, connecting spoken words to written forms
target_vocab: words learner already knows spoken, now in script: baba, mama, bayt, akl, shukran
heritage.topic: Learning to read Arabic by starting with words you already know how to say
heritage.why: Heritage Arabic speakers often have zero literacy. Starting with familiar spoken vocabulary makes script learning immediately meaningful rather than abstract.
heritage.warm_up: Learner says 10 Arabic words they know — tutor writes them in Arabic script, learner starts matching sound to symbol
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Take an inventory of words the learner already knows from home. Write them in Arabic script. Have learner match spoken to written. Teach letter recognition through these known words rather than through the alphabet in order. This leverages existing oral knowledge as scaffolding for literacy. Focus on the most frequent letters first. Every word read is a word they already own.
assessment_targets: Can read 5+ familiar words in Arabic script
Can identify 10+ letters from context
Connects spoken vocabulary to written forms

heritage.title: Kitchen Arabic → Real Arabic
heritage.target_grammar: expanding vocabulary domains, MSA vs dialectal awareness
target_vocab: MSA equivalents of dialectal words learner uses
heritage.topic: Moving from household Arabic to academic and professional vocabulary
heritage.why: Heritage speakers plateau at kitchen/family vocabulary. Bridging to MSA and academic vocabulary opens doors to media, education, and professional Arabic use.
heritage.warm_up: Vocabulary audit: which life domains can learner discuss in Arabic? Food? School? Work? Science? Politics?
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Map the learner's existing dialect vocabulary, then bridge to MSA equivalents where they differ. Introduce new vocabulary domains: school (jami'a, imtihan, mudarris), work (shughul, wathiqa, taqrir), current events. For each new word, check if learner knows a dialectal version. This lesson is about expansion, not replacement — dialect is valid, MSA adds range. Build vocabulary through topic-based conversation, not word lists.
assessment_targets: Can identify 3+ differences between their dialect and MSA
Knows 5+ academic vocabulary words
Can discuss school/work topics in Arabic

heritage.title: Verb Forms — The System You Already Know
heritage.target_grammar: making verb conjugation conscious — past and present for ana/anta/huwa
heritage.topic: Systematizing verb knowledge — you already use these forms, now understand the pattern
heritage.why: Heritage speakers conjugate verbs by feel. Making the pattern conscious fills in gaps (they might say 'katab' correctly but stumble on 'yaktubun') and enables self-correction.
heritage.warm_up: Verb drill: tutor gives infinitive meaning, learner tries to produce all forms they can
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Start by showing learner what they already know: have them conjugate a familiar verb and point out the patterns. Then introduce the systematic pattern for Group I verbs. Focus on the 3 most useful persons: ana (I), anta/anti (you), huwa/hiya (he/she). Use verbs from daily life. Identify which conjugations they get right by instinct and which they guess at. Fill gaps systematically. This is consciousness-raising, not new learning.
assessment_targets: Can conjugate 2+ verbs in present tense for 3 persons
Can conjugate 2+ verbs in past tense for 3 persons
Identifies own conjugation gaps

heritage.title: Writing Your First Paragraph
heritage.target_grammar: connected script writing, basic punctuation, sentence connectors
heritage.topic: Writing in Arabic script — from words to sentences to a short paragraph
heritage.why: Heritage speakers need to cross from oral-only to literate. Writing a paragraph about a familiar topic proves that literacy is achievable and practical.
heritage.warm_up: Learner writes the 5 Arabic words they are most confident about, then tutor helps build them into sentences
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Build up from words → phrases → sentences → paragraph. Start with words learner can already read from lesson 2. Add connectors (wa = and, lakinna = but, li'anna = because). Have learner write 3-5 sentences about their family or daily routine. Focus on connected letter forms — Arabic is cursive by default. Check right-to-left flow. This is a milestone lesson — producing a paragraph is a major confidence builder.
assessment_targets: Can write 3+ connected sentences in Arabic script
Uses at least 2 connectors
Maintains right-to-left writing correctly

heritage.title: Understanding the News
heritage.target_grammar: MSA listening comprehension, formal vocabulary, passive voice recognition
heritage.topic: Listening to and reading Arabic news — Al Jazeera, BBC Arabic headlines
heritage.why: Arabic news is in MSA — the universal formal register. Heritage speakers who only know dialect cannot access this. News comprehension unlocks media, information, and cultural connection.
heritage.warm_up: Listen to a 30-second Al Jazeera clip together, learner identifies every word they recognize
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Play a short, simple news clip. First pass: learner identifies any words they catch. Second pass: tutor pauses and explains. Third pass: learner summarizes in their own Arabic (dialect is fine). Introduce common news vocabulary. Show a headline in Arabic script — read together. The goal is comprehension, not production of MSA. Bridge from dialect understanding to MSA understanding.
assessment_targets: Can identify main topic of a simple news clip
Recognizes 5+ news vocabulary words
Can read and understand an Arabic headline

heritage.title: Dialect Pride — Understanding Your Family's Arabic
heritage.target_grammar: systematic dialect vs MSA comparison, phonological patterns
heritage.topic: Mapping the dialect — what makes your family's Arabic unique and how does it relate to MSA?
heritage.why: Heritage speakers often feel their Arabic is 'wrong' because it is not MSA. Understanding dialect as a legitimate system builds confidence and reveals the MSA underneath.
heritage.warm_up: Record (or recall) 5 things the learner's family says at home — analyze together
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Have learner share phrases from home. Map dialect features systematically: sound changes (qaf → g/'), vocabulary differences, structural patterns. Show that these are regular, rule-governed differences, not errors. Compare with MSA equivalents. Discuss which Arabic-speaking region their family's dialect comes from. This lesson validates the learner's home language while building linguistic awareness. It should feel empowering.
assessment_targets: Can identify 3+ systematic differences between their dialect and MSA
Can explain the 'same word' in both dialect and MSA
Shows metalinguistic awareness of their own Arabic variety

heritage.title: Formal Arabic for Real Life
heritage.target_grammar: formal request structures, polite forms, official document vocabulary
heritage.topic: Writing formal letters, understanding official documents, making formal phone calls
heritage.why: Heritage speakers handle casual Arabic fine but freeze at formal contexts: government forms, formal letters, professional communication. This lesson builds real-world formal competency.
heritage.warm_up: Scenario: you need to write a formal letter in Arabic requesting information from an embassy
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Teach the structure of a formal Arabic letter: opening (al-sayyid al-muhtaram), body (arjuu an..., ataqaddam bi...), closing (tafaddaluu bi-qabul). Practice with realistic scenarios: writing to an embassy, responding to a formal invitation, understanding a government document. Contrast with how learner would say the same thing informally. Build templates learner can reuse. This is high-value practical Arabic.
assessment_targets: Can write formal letter opening and closing
Uses 3+ formal expressions correctly
Can distinguish formal from informal in written Arabic

heritage.title: Arabic Poetry and Proverbs
heritage.target_grammar: classical Arabic structures, rhythmic patterns, figurative language
heritage.topic: Arabic proverbs, poetry snippets, and cultural wisdom — connecting to literary tradition
heritage.why: Arabic has one of the world's richest poetic traditions. Proverbs are culturally essential and linguistically rich. Heritage speakers may know some from family — this lesson connects personal to literary.
heritage.warm_up: Does learner know any Arabic proverbs or sayings? Collect and analyze together.
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Start from what learner knows: family sayings, religious phrases, cultural expressions. Introduce famous Arabic proverbs (al-sabru miftahu al-faraj = patience is the key to relief). Analyze structure and meaning. Read a short classical poem snippet — show how roots they know appear in poetic context. Discuss rhyme and rhythm in Arabic poetry (qafiya). Connect to the cultural weight of Arabic as a language of literature and revelation.
assessment_targets: Can explain meaning of 3+ Arabic proverbs
Recognizes root patterns in literary language
Can recite one proverb from memory

heritage.title: Building a Study Plan — Where Do You Go From Here?
heritage.target_grammar: self-assessment in Arabic, goal-setting vocabulary, resource navigation
heritage.topic: Self-assessment, goal-setting, and finding resources for continued Arabic development
heritage.why: Capstone lesson. Heritage refreshers need a forward path: which resources work for their level? What should they focus on next? This lesson builds autonomous learning skills.
heritage.warm_up: Reflection conversation in Arabic: what was hard, what was easy, what surprised you in these 10 lessons?
heritage.instructions_for_tutor: Conduct lesson primarily in Arabic at whatever level learner can sustain. Review progress across 10 lessons — what can they do now that they could not before? Identify remaining gaps together. Discuss resources: Al Jazeera Learning, Arabic pods, family conversation practice, mosque community. Help set 3 concrete goals. Have learner write their goals in Arabic. This should feel like graduation, not an ending. In Arabic: hadhihi al-bidaya faqat (this is only the beginning).
assessment_targets: Can self-assess strengths and weaknesses in Arabic
Sets 3 concrete goals in Arabic
Can sustain a 5-minute conversation in Arabic about learning

beginner.title: Hajimemashite — First Contact
beginner.target_grammar: self-introduction formula, desu/masu polite form
beginner.topic: The Japanese self-introduction ritual — name, status, polite closing
beginner.why: Japanese self-introductions are formulaic and culturally essential. The pattern hajimemashite → [name] desu → yoroshiku onegaishimasu is a complete social ritual to master from day one.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor performs the full self-introduction, then breaks it down piece by piece for learner
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach the full jikoshoukai (self-introduction) as a memorized script first, then unpack. Hajimemashite = nice to meet you (first time), [name] desu = I am [name], yoroshiku onegaishimasu = please treat me well. Do not explain desu grammatically yet — treat it as a chunk. Practice until learner can deliver smoothly. Explain the cultural significance: this formula is used in every first meeting in Japan.
assessment_targets: Can perform full self-introduction without prompting
Pronunciation of hajimemashite is comprehensible
Uses desu correctly after name

beginner.title: Hiragana — The First Writing System
beginner.target_grammar: hiragana a-ko rows (25 characters), reading simple words
target_vocab: a-i-u-e-o row through ka-ki-ku-ke-ko through sa-shi-su-se-so through ta-chi-tsu-te-to through na-ni-nu-ne-no
beginner.topic: Learning the first 25 hiragana — reading Japanese for the first time
beginner.why: Hiragana is non-negotiable for Japanese. Unlike Arabic's one script, Japanese has three — start early and make it routine. Anime watchers can recognize these in credits and subtitles.
beginner.warm_up: Show anime titles in hiragana — learner guesses which show based on any characters they recognize
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach hiragana in the traditional a-i-u-e-o vowel order, 5 characters per row. Use mnemonics for each character shape. Practice reading simple words immediately: ashi (foot), inu (dog), sushi, tofu. Connect to anime where possible. Do not rush — 25 characters is plenty for one lesson. End with a reading test: 5 simple words in hiragana. Recommend daily practice app (write by hand).
assessment_targets: Can read 20+ of the first 25 hiragana
Can read 3+ simple words in hiragana
Can write own name in hiragana (approximation)

beginner.title: Hiragana — Complete Set + Dakuten
beginner.target_grammar: remaining hiragana (ha through wo/n), dakuten and handakuten
target_vocab: ha-hi-fu-he-ho through ma-mi-mu-me-mo through ya-yu-yo through ra-ri-ru-re-ro through wa-wo-n
beginner.topic: Completing hiragana mastery — all 46 base characters plus voiced variations
beginner.why: Complete hiragana unlocks all Japanese pronunciation. Dakuten (voicing marks) double the usable character set with a simple rule. This is the literacy foundation everything builds on.
beginner.warm_up: Review first 25 hiragana with speed drill, then introduce remaining characters in groups
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Complete the hiragana chart. Teach the dakuten pattern: ka→ga, sa→za, ta→da, ha→ba (two dots = voiced). Handakuten: ha→pa (circle). This systematic pattern is a shortcut — learner already knows the base character. Practice reading longer words: tabemono (food), nihongo (Japanese language), arigatou. Start reading simple sentences in hiragana. Assign daily writing practice.
assessment_targets: Can read all 46 base hiragana
Understands dakuten voicing pattern
Can read a simple hiragana sentence

beginner.title: Kore wa Nan Desu Ka? — What Is This?
beginner.target_grammar: X wa Y desu sentence pattern, ka question particle, kore/sore/are demonstratives
beginner.topic: Identifying objects, asking and answering 'what is this?'
beginner.why: X wa Y desu is THE fundamental Japanese sentence pattern. Mastering it with demonstratives provides a framework for expressing almost anything at beginner level.
beginner.warm_up: Point at objects: tutor says kore wa [word] desu, learner guesses meaning from context
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach the core pattern: kore wa [noun] desu = this is a [noun]. Add ka for questions: kore wa nan desu ka = what is this? Introduce the three-way demonstrative system: kore (near me), sore (near you), are (far from both) — contrast with English's two-way this/that. Practice with classroom/room objects. Build confidence with the pattern before adding complexity. Use hiragana in written examples.
assessment_targets: Can ask kore wa nan desu ka
Can answer with X wa Y desu pattern
Distinguishes kore/sore/are correctly

beginner.title: Counting Things (and Why It's Weird)
beginner.target_grammar: numbers 1-100, counter words (tsu, nin, hon, mai), hitotsu system
beginner.topic: Numbers and the Japanese counter word system — why you cannot just say 'three'
beginner.why: Counter words are uniquely Japanese and endlessly confusing for beginners. Early systematic exposure prevents fossilized errors. The hitotsu system is the universal fallback.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor holds up fingers, learner says the number. Then: why can you not just say 'san cats'?
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach numbers 1-10, then the tens (juu, nijuu, sanjuu...) up to 100. Introduce the concept of counter words: different counters for flat things (mai), long things (hon), people (nin), general (tsu). Teach the hitotsu-futatsu native Japanese counting system as the universal fallback. Focus on the 3 most useful counters (nin, hon, mai) plus the general tsu system. Use anime examples: Naruto has nana-nin no shinobi (seven ninja).
assessment_targets: Can count 1-10 fluently
Knows the hitotsu-futatsu system for 1-5
Can use at least 2 counter words correctly

beginner.title: Katakana — Foreign Words in Japanese
beginner.target_grammar: katakana full chart, loan word patterns, long vowels in katakana
beginner.topic: Learning katakana through English loan words — instant vocabulary boost
beginner.why: Katakana is used for foreign words, and Japanese is full of English loans. Learning katakana gives an immediate vocabulary of hundreds of words George already knows in English.
beginner.warm_up: Decode game: tutor writes English words in katakana, learner sounds them out and guesses the English
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach katakana as the 'foreign word script.' Start with obvious English loans: koohii (coffee), terebi (TV), pasokon (personal computer). Have learner decode words by sounding out katakana — this is a puzzle game. Teach the full chart systematically but keep returning to the decoding game. Introduce long vowel marks (ー) used in katakana. This lesson should feel like gaining superpowers — suddenly hundreds of words are readable.
assessment_targets: Can read 30+ katakana characters
Can decode 5+ English loan words from katakana
Understands the function of katakana vs hiragana

beginner.title: Where Are You Going? — Particles and Movement
beginner.target_grammar: particles wa/ga/wo/ni/e/de, ikimasu/kimasu/kaerimasu
beginner.topic: Describing movement and location — particles as the skeleton of Japanese
beginner.why: Particles are what make Japanese Japanese. They mark grammatical relationships without word order. This lesson cracks the particle code through movement verbs, which make the spatial meaning concrete.
beginner.warm_up: Sentence building blocks: tutor gives words + particles as puzzle pieces, learner assembles sentences
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Focus on particles as markers: ni = destination/time, de = location of action, wo = direct object, wa = topic. Teach through movement verbs: gakkou ni ikimasu (go to school), uchi de tabemasu (eat at home), eki e kaerimasu (return toward the station). Use physical movement or diagrams to make particles spatial and concrete. Do not overload — master these 4 particles before adding more. Particles are the single most important grammar point in Japanese.
assessment_targets: Can use ni for destination
Can use de for location of action
Can form 3+ sentences with correct particle use

beginner.title: What Do You Like? — Expressing Preferences
beginner.target_grammar: suki/kirai + ga particle, degree expressions (totemo, amari, zenzen)
beginner.topic: Likes, dislikes, strengths, weaknesses — expressing personal preferences
beginner.why: Suki/kirai are among the most used words in anime and daily Japanese. The ga particle here is different from other languages' approaches to 'like' — important structural lesson.
beginner.warm_up: Anime preferences: tutor asks about favorite shows using suki, learner recognizes from anime exposure
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Teach suki desu as a complete preference expression. Note the structure difference: X ga suki desu = 'X is liked (by me)' — the thing liked takes ga, not wo. This is counterintuitive for English speakers. Practice with anime, food, school subjects. Add degree: totemo suki (really like), amari suki ja nai (don't really like), zenzen suki ja nai (don't like at all). Connect to anime scenes where characters say 'suki' — learner will recognize it immediately.
assessment_targets: Can express likes with X ga suki desu
Can express dislikes with X ga kirai desu
Uses degree expressions (totemo, amari) correctly

beginner.title: Yesterday, Today, Tomorrow — Time and Tense
beginner.target_grammar: masu → mashita past tense, masen negative, time expressions
beginner.topic: Talking about past actions and future plans using the masu conjugation system
beginner.why: The masu system is beautifully regular — masu (present), mashita (past), masen (negative), masen deshita (past negative). Four forms unlock all basic tense/polarity combinations.
beginner.warm_up: Tutor describes yesterday in Japanese: kinou, toshokan ni ikimashita. Learner identifies the past tense marker.
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Present the masu conjugation paradigm as a simple 2x2 grid: present/past x positive/negative. This is one of the most elegant parts of Japanese grammar — celebrate its regularity. Practice by having learner describe yesterday (mashita forms), then plans for tomorrow (masu forms), then things they did not do (masen/masen deshita). Use time words as anchors. Compare to anime: characters often drop masu for casual speech (tabeta vs tabemashita) — acknowledge but keep to polite forms for now.
assessment_targets: Can form past tense with mashita
Can form negative with masen
Can narrate yesterday in 3+ sentences

beginner.title: Putting It All Together — A Day in Tokyo
beginner.target_grammar: combining all grammar points, sentence chaining with te-form introduction
beginner.topic: Narrating a day — combining particles, tenses, preferences, and connectors into continuous speech
beginner.why: Capstone lesson. Integration of all skills into sustained communication. The te-form introduction (verb chaining) is a bridge to intermediate Japanese and the key to sounding natural.
beginner.warm_up: Roleplay: learner plans and narrates a dream day in Tokyo — where would they go, what would they eat, who would they see?
beginner.instructions_for_tutor: Have learner plan and describe a fictional day in Tokyo using everything learned: movement verbs + particles (Akihabara ni ikimashita), preferences (ramen ga daisuki desu), past tense (anime wo mimashita), numbers/counters. Introduce te-form as a sentence connector: tabete, nonde, itte... (ate, then drank, then went...). Keep te-form light — just 3-4 common verbs. Evaluate overall progress. Celebrate how far learner has come from hajimemashite. Note areas for future study.
assessment_targets: Can narrate 6+ connected sentences about a day
Uses particles, tenses, and preferences correctly
Attempts te-form for at least 2 verbs

passive_exposure.title: Anime Phrases You Already Know
passive_exposure.target_grammar: identifying casual vs polite speech, recognizing sentence-final particles
passive_exposure.topic: Auditing your anime vocabulary — what you actually know and what it really means
passive_exposure.why: George already has hundreds of Japanese words from anime. This lesson activates that passive knowledge, corrects misconceptions, and maps what he knows to actual grammar.
passive_exposure.warm_up: Rapid fire: tutor says anime phrases, learner gives meaning. How many does learner actually know?
passive_exposure.instructions_for_tutor: Take inventory of every Japanese word/phrase the learner knows from anime. List them. For each one: confirm meaning, explain register (casual/rude/polite), note when it is appropriate vs not. Many anime phrases are rude in real life (omae, kisama, temee) — explain this cultural gap. Group by function: exclamations, commands, reactions, questions. This lesson transforms passive hearing into conscious knowledge. Build a 'your anime vocabulary' reference list.
assessment_targets: Can define 10+ anime-sourced Japanese words
Can identify register level (casual/polite/rude) for 5+ phrases
Understands that anime speech ≠ polite Japanese

passive_exposure.title: Attack on Titan — Military Commands and Determination
passive_exposure.target_grammar: imperative forms, volitional (let's/I will), informal past tense
passive_exposure.topic: Attack on Titan vocabulary deep dive — commands, willpower, humanity
passive_exposure.why: AoT is rich in imperative and volitional forms that standard textbooks delay. Analyzing familiar scenes activates production from recognition. The emotional weight of these scenes aids memory.
passive_exposure.warm_up: Play/describe an iconic AoT scene: what did you hear? What did it mean?
passive_exposure.instructions_for_tutor: Use AoT as the textbook. Susume (advance!) → explain imperative form. Tatakae (fight!) → show how tatakau (to fight) becomes tatakae (command). Shinzou wo sasageyo (dedicate your hearts) → break down particle wo and volitional form. Connect emotions to grammar: why does Eren say tatakae and not tatakaimasu? Register and intensity. Have learner practice producing these forms with new verbs. Build grammar understanding through beloved content.
assessment_targets: Can identify imperative form in 3+ verbs
Can explain meaning of 5+ AoT phrases
Understands volitional form (you/sasageyo) conceptually

passive_exposure.title: Death Note — The Language of Strategy
passive_exposure.target_grammar: conditional forms (tara/ba), keikaku (plan) vocabulary, thinking verbs
passive_exposure.topic: Death Note vocabulary — strategy, morality, intellectual language
passive_exposure.why: Death Note is linguistically rich with conditional reasoning, moral vocabulary, and inner monologue. Light's speeches use grammar patterns that illuminate how Japanese expresses complex thought.
passive_exposure.warm_up: Iconic line analysis: 'Keikaku doori' (Just as planned / All according to keikaku) — what does each word mean?
passive_exposure.instructions_for_tutor: Death Note gives access to intellectual Japanese: thinking verbs (kangaeru = think, shiru = know, wakaru = understand), moral vocabulary (seigi = justice, tsumi = sin/crime), and conditional forms (kono nooto ni namae wo kaketara, sono hito wa shinu = if you write a name in this notebook, that person dies). Break down Light and L's key phrases. Show how conditional tara form works through Death Note's rules. This show teaches Japanese logic and argumentation patterns.
assessment_targets: Can explain meaning of 5+ Death Note phrases
Recognizes tara conditional pattern
Knows thinking/knowing verbs: kangaeru, shiru, wakaru

passive_exposure.title: Isekai Vocabulary — Another World, Same Grammar
passive_exposure.target_grammar: potential form (can do), RPG/game vocabulary that maps to real grammar
passive_exposure.topic: Isekai and shonen vocabulary — power systems, abilities, and the potential form
passive_exposure.why: RPG-style anime uses potential forms constantly (I can fight, I can't win). These map directly to real Japanese grammar. Fantasy vocabulary is fun scaffolding for the potential form.
passive_exposure.warm_up: Anime power scaling: tutor uses potential form — Goku wa tatakaeru (Goku can fight), learner identifies the pattern
passive_exposure.instructions_for_tutor: Use isekai/shonen concepts to teach the potential form. Taberu → taberareru (can eat), tatakau → tatakaeru (can fight), miru → mirareru (can see). Show the pattern for Group 1 and Group 2 verbs. Fantasy vocabulary provides the content: mahou ga tsukaeru (can use magic), teki ni kateru (can win against the enemy). Practice with 'can you...?' questions about real life: nihongo ga hanasemasu ka? (can you speak Japanese?). The potential form is high-frequency and immediately useful.
assessment_targets: Can form potential form for 3+ verbs
Knows 5+ isekai/shonen vocabulary words
Can ask and answer 'can you...?' questions

passive_exposure.title: Reading the Screen — Kanji From Anime
passive_exposure.target_grammar: first 20 kanji from anime titles and common on-screen text
passive_exposure.topic: Learning kanji through anime titles, attack names, and on-screen text you have already seen hundreds of times
passive_exposure.why: George has been staring at these kanji in anime titles and subtitles for years without reading them. Connecting visual recognition to meaning is the fastest path to kanji acquisition.
passive_exposure.warm_up: Show anime title cards in Japanese: Shingeki no Kyojin (進撃の巨人), Death Note (デスノート), can learner identify any characters?
passive_exposure.instructions_for_tutor: Select 20 high-frequency kanji that appear in anime titles and common phrases. For each: show the kanji, give the meaning, connect to an anime the learner knows. 進撃 (shingeki = advance/attack), 巨人 (kyojin = giant), 死 (shi = death), 神 (kami = god), 人 (jin/hito = person). Show how kanji compound: 巨 (giant) + 人 (person) = 巨人 (titan). This compositional logic is the key to kanji. Assign 5 kanji per week for practice. Focus on recognition, not writing.
assessment_targets: Can recognize and give meaning for 10+ kanji
Can read 3+ anime titles in kanji
Understands how kanji compounds work (A+B=AB meaning)

passive_exposure.title: Casual Speech Patterns — How Characters Actually Talk
passive_exposure.target_grammar: dictionary form vs masu form, casual conjugation, sentence-final particles (yo, ne, na, ze, wa)
passive_exposure.topic: Decoding casual Japanese from anime — what textbooks skip and anime teaches
passive_exposure.why: Textbooks teach masu form. Anime uses dictionary form and casual speech. Bridging this gap is essential for comprehension and reveals the underlying verb system.
passive_exposure.warm_up: Listen to an anime clip: identify every word that sounds different from textbook Japanese
passive_exposure.instructions_for_tutor: Show the polite → casual mapping: tabemasu → taberu, ikimasu → iku, shimasu → suru, desu → da. Explain that anime characters speak casual Japanese (dictionary form + sentence particles). Teach sentence-final particles: yo (assertion), ne (agreement-seeking), na (male casual), ze (rough masculine), wa (feminine/emphatic). Practice converting masu sentences to casual: gakkou ni ikimasu → gakkou ni iku. Explain gendered speech patterns in anime vs real life. This lesson unlocks anime comprehension dramatically.
assessment_targets: Can convert 5+ masu-form verbs to dictionary form
Can identify 3+ sentence-final particles and their function
Understands polite vs casual register distinction

passive_exposure.title: Emotions and Reactions — The Expressive Core
passive_exposure.topic: Japanese emotion words and adjective system — the vocabulary that makes anime dramatic
passive_exposure.why: Anime is emotionally expressive. Japanese has rich emotion vocabulary that learner already recognizes aurally. Formalizing i-adj vs na-adj through emotional vocabulary makes the grammar stick.
passive_exposure.warm_up: Emotion charades: tutor says Japanese emotion word, learner acts it out. Then reverse.
passive_exposure.instructions_for_tutor: Teach the two adjective types through emotions: i-adjectives (ureshii, kanashii, kowai, tanoshii — all end in -i) and na-adjectives (kirei-na, hen-na, fushigi-na — need na before nouns). Show conjugation of i-adjectives: ureshii → ureshikunai (not happy) → ureshikatta (was happy). Connect every word to anime scenes: kowai = what characters say when titans appear, sugoi = universal exclamation. Teach yabai as the modern all-purpose exclamation (good or bad, like English 'sick'). These words are already in learner's ear — formalize them.
assessment_targets: Can use 5+ emotion adjectives correctly
Can distinguish i-adjective from na-adjective
Can negate an i-adjective (-kunai form)

passive_exposure.title: Te-form — The Key That Unlocks Everything
passive_exposure.target_grammar: te-form conjugation, te-iru (ongoing), te-kudasai (please do), te-mo ii (may I)
passive_exposure.topic: The te-form and its many powers — connecting, requesting, describing, permitting
passive_exposure.why: Te-form is the most important conjugation in Japanese. It chains actions, makes requests, describes ongoing states, and asks permission. Anime is full of te-form — learner will hear it everywhere once aware.
passive_exposure.warm_up: Anime command recognition: chotto matte (wait a sec) → matte is te-form of matsu. What other te-forms does learner recognize?
passive_exposure.instructions_for_tutor: Teach te-form conjugation rules (the tte/nde/ite/ide patterns for Group 1, te for Group 2, shite/kite for irregulars). Then show its power: te-kudasai = please do (tabete kudasai = please eat), te-iru = ongoing (tabete-iru = eating right now), te-mo ii = may I? (tabete-mo ii? = may I eat?). Each te-form use is a separate grammar point in textbooks — here they are unified. Use anime examples: yamete! = te-form of yameru (stop) used as command. Drill the conjugation until it is automatic.
assessment_targets: Can form te-form for 5+ common verbs
Can make a polite request with te-kudasai
Can describe an ongoing action with te-iru

passive_exposure.title: Understanding Plot — Listening Comprehension Level Up
passive_exposure.target_grammar: relative clauses, quoting with to, complex sentence structures
passive_exposure.topic: Following anime dialogue without subtitles — understanding who said what, why, and when
passive_exposure.why: This lesson bridges word recognition to sentence comprehension. Quoting (to), reason (kara/node), and time (toki) markers are what hold Japanese narrative together.
passive_exposure.warm_up: Watch a 2-minute anime clip with Japanese subtitles. Learner identifies every word/structure they recognize. Count the percentage.
passive_exposure.instructions_for_tutor: Teach narrative connectors: to omou (I think that...), to itta (said that...), kara/node (because), toki (when), noni (even though). These are the glue of Japanese storytelling. Practice by analyzing anime dialogue: break complex sentences into parts. Show how relative clauses work in Japanese (they come before the noun, opposite of English). Test comprehension with a dialogue passage — can learner follow the plot in Japanese? This lesson should significantly boost anime comprehension.
assessment_targets: Can identify quoting pattern (to omou/to iu) in dialogue
Can use kara/node for giving reasons
Can follow main plot points in a 2-minute anime clip

passive_exposure.title: Subtitle-Free Challenge
passive_exposure.target_grammar: comprehensive review through media immersion, self-assessment
target_vocab: all previously learned vocabulary in context
passive_exposure.topic: Watch an anime episode without subtitles — how much can you understand now?
passive_exposure.why: Capstone lesson. This is the goal George stated: watching anime without subtitles. Testing against real content measures true progress and identifies the next learning frontier.
passive_exposure.warm_up: Choose a familiar anime episode (one learner has seen before in English). This time: Japanese audio, no subtitles.
passive_exposure.instructions_for_tutor: This is an immersion test and celebration. Select an episode of a show learner knows well (AoT or Death Note). Watch 10-15 minutes with Japanese audio, no subtitles. Pause every 2-3 minutes: what did you understand? What did you miss? Categorize misses: vocabulary gap, grammar gap, speed gap, or accent gap. After viewing, review 5 key lines from the episode — break them down completely. Set goals for continued learning. Calculate approximate comprehension percentage. Celebrate progress from lesson 1 to now. Frame this as the beginning of the intermediate journey, not an ending.
assessment_targets: Can identify main plot events from audio alone
Can transcribe 3+ complete sentences heard in the episode
Self-assesses comprehension percentage honestly
